title: The Fifth Sunday after the Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver | February 9, 2020 | Broderick Greer

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This sermon falls on the Fifth Sunday after the Epiphany in the liturgical calendar, situated in the latter part of the Epiphany season before Lent begins on Ash Wednesday (February 26, 2020). The RCL readings for this Sunday would have been Isaiah 40:21-31, Psalm 147:1-11, 20c, 1 Corinthians 9:24-27, and Mark 1:29-39, focusing on themes of divine power and endurance in faith. The church was in Ordinary Time, specifically the post-Epiphany period emphasizing Christ's manifestation and the calling of disciples.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the quiet period between the 79th General Convention (held in 2018 in Austin, Texas) and the upcoming 80th General Convention scheduled for July 2021 in Baltimore. The Diocese of Colorado was under the leadership of Bishop Robert W. Imhoff Jr., who served from 2010-2020 (he would retire later that year), representing a stable diocesan period without major controversies or bishop elections at this moment. No major denominational controversies or General Convention interim actions were dominating church news in early February 2020.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical winter conditions in early February, with the previous week marked by relatively mild temperatures and no major snow events disrupting the metropolitan area. The Colorado state legislature had convened on January 8, 2020, for its 120-day session, with Democratic majorities addressing healthcare, education funding, and environmental issues. The Denver metro area was largely focused on local governance issues and school board matters rather than major crises, creating a relatively stable civic environment for the Cathedral's work.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

President Donald Trump had just been acquitted in his impeachment trial on February 5, 2020 (four days before this sermon), a watershed political moment dividing the nation. The Democratic primary race for the 2020 presidential election was intensifying, with Iowa's caucuses having just occurred on February 3, creating intense campaign activity and media focus. The COVID-19 pandemic was emerging as a concern with the first confirmed US death reported on February 6, 2020, though it had not yet dominated national consciousness or disrupted American life as it would within weeks.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

China was in the midst of the rapidly escalating COVID-19 outbreak centered in Wuhan, with the disease beginning its global spread despite lockdowns implemented in late January; by February 9, over 37,000 cases had been confirmed in China. The U.S.-China "Phase One" trade deal had just been signed on January 15, 2020, representing a temporary stabilization of trade tensions amid the emerging pandemic. The Middle East remained tense following the January 3 assassination of Iranian General Qasem Soleimani and subsequent Iranian missile strikes on U.S. bases in Iraq, though the acute crisis had cooled by February.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

America was in a state of political polarization following the impeachment trial, with deep divisions about presidential accountability and constitutional norms creating tension across families and communities. The cultural mood was one of pre-pandemic normalcy—Super Bowl LIV had just aired on February 2, 2020, with the Kansas City Chiefs defeating the San Francisco 49ers, providing a unifying national entertainment moment. The prevailing zeitgeist was one of routine life and political engagement, with few Americans aware that the novel coronavirus would fundamentally disrupt normal life, church gatherings, and worship within a matter of weeks.
